sources: ISSN Position Stand: Probiotics and the Athlete (JISSN, 2019) — doi:10.1186/s12970-019-0327-2
de Oliveira EP et al: Gastrointestinal Complaints During Exercise (Sports Med, 2014)
NIH NIDDK: Eating, Diet, and Nutrition for Irritable Bowel Syndrome
ACSM: Nutrition and Athletic Performance (Med Sci Sports Exerc, 2016)
note: GI distress is one of the most common reasons athletes underperform or abandon training plans. Most cases are manageable through food timing and composition.

gi_distress_during_exercise.who_is_at_risk: Runners (mechanical jostling), cyclists (prolonged aero position), high-intensity athletes, anyone with pre-existing IBS or food intolerances.
main_causes: High-fibre foods eaten too close to training (fermentation, gas)
High-fat meals pre-workout (slow gastric emptying, blood diverted to digestion)
Fructose or sorbitol in pre-workout foods (apples, pears, protein bars with sugar alcohols)

timing_rules.2_3_hours_before: Last full meal. Moderate fibre, moderate fat, good carb + protein content.
timing_rules.1_hour_before: Small snack only. Low fibre, low fat, easily digestible carbs. E.g. banana, plain rice cake, dates.
timing_rules.30_min_before: If anything: a few dates or a small banana. Nothing high in fat, fibre, or protein.
foods_to_avoid_pre_training: Raw vegetables and salads (high fibre, fermentation)
Legumes (dal, chickpeas, kidney beans) — fine at dinner, avoid 2 hrs pre-training
High-fat foods (fried, cheese, avocado in large amounts)
Artificial sweeteners (sorbitol, xylitol) in protein bars or sugar-free products

gut_microbiome_and_performance.why_it_matters: A diverse gut microbiome improves nutrient absorption, reduces inflammation, and supports immune function — all relevant for training adaptation.

probiotics.evidence: Moderate (ISSN 2019). Most benefit seen in reducing upper respiratory infections in athletes and managing GI symptoms.
probiotics.note: Fermented Indian foods like idli, dosa, dhokla, and kanji are naturally probiotic-rich and widely available.

prebiotics.why: Feed beneficial gut bacteria. Found in fibre-rich whole foods.
prebiotics.note: Increase prebiotic fibre gradually — sudden large increases cause bloating.

gut_microbiome_and_performance.diversity_principle: Aim for 30+ different plant foods per week to support microbiome diversity. Variety matters more than any single 'superfood'.

common_causes: Post-workout protein shake with lactose or artificial sweeteners
Eating too quickly after training (swallowing air)
fixes: Switch to plant-based protein (pea/rice) without sweeteners
Chew slowly, don't rush the post-workout meal
If a specific food repeatedly causes bloating, track and remove it for 2 weeks

ibs_and_exercise.note: If symptoms are frequent, severe, or include blood, refer to a doctor — not a dietary adjustment issue.
ibs_and_exercise.low_fodmap_principle: For IBS athletes, a temporary low-FODMAP trial (under dietitian guidance) can identify trigger foods. High-FODMAP foods include onion, garlic, wheat, apple, high-lactose dairy, legumes in large portions.
